title: Expansion, Divisibility and Parity
speaker: Harald Helfgott
abstract: We will discuss a graph that encodes the divisibility properties of integers by primes. We will prove that this graph has a strong local expander property almost everywhere. We then obtain several consequences in number theory, beyond the traditional parity barrier, by combining the main result with Matomaki-Radziwill. (This is joint work with M. Radziwill.) For instance: for lambda the Liouville function (that is, the completely multiplicative function with lambda(p)=−1 for every prime), (1/logx)∑n≤xlambda(n)λ(n+1)/n=O(

proof_stages.label: Liouville parity correlations and Tao’s reduction
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates the parity problem through the Liouville function, recalls that its averages are tied to the prime number theorem while its two-point correlations are open, and presents Matomäki-Radziwill and Tao’s reduction to averaged shifted correlations over primes with the condition p divides n.

proof_stages.label: Divisibility graph as a local expansion problem
proof_stages.summary: A graph is defined on integers in an interval with edges of prime length p when p divides a vertex, and the desired replacement of divisibility by weight 1/p is reformulated as a local, almost-everywhere spectral expansion bound for the difference between this graph and its naive weighted model.

proof_stages.label: Uniform spectral theorem and arithmetic consequences
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem asserts that for any admissible set of primes there is a large vertex subset on which every eigenvalue of the restricted difference operator is essentially as small as square-root of the average degree, yielding improved logarithmic Chowla-type corollaries and almost-all-scales variants.

proof_stages.label: Large eigenvalues force a large trace
proof_stages.summary: Locality replaces group symmetry by showing that a surviving large eigenvalue produces many large eigenvalues, so a high even power trace must be large and the spectral theorem reduces to bounding weighted closed paths.

proof_stages.label: Cancellation after pruning bad vertices
proof_stages.summary: Trace terms are shown to cancel when many edge labels appear only once, but first one removes high-degree vertices, short cycles, and early recurrences, proving via number-theoretic sieve estimates with composite moduli that the removed set is small and well distributed.

proof_stages.label: Closed-path shapes and divisibility constraints
proof_stages.summary: For the remaining closed paths, most prime labels recur, and each recurrence forces a divisibility relation, so after fixing the path’s shape and signs the counting problem becomes a system of matrix-like divisibility conditions on prime tuples.

proof_stages.label: Combinatorial rank and geometry-of-numbers count
proof_stages.summary: A reduced graph of equivalence classes and a blue-red partition with a large boundary supply a high-rank subsystem for most shapes, after which elementary geometry of numbers and brutal linear algebra bound the number of admissible tuples while the low-rank shapes are shown to be rare.

proof_stages.label: Rough-modulus extension and further uniformity directions
proof_stages.summary: The same strategy is extended in Pilatte’s work from prime moduli to rough composite moduli by replacing rank arguments with explicit independent vectors, and the speaker then discusses possible phase-weight generalizations and the obstacles to higher-correlation hypergraph analogues.

examples.name: Complete graph model for ordinary expansion
examples.note: The complete graph is used as a reality check showing that the usual expander spectral gap can be rephrased as bounding the adjacency operator after subtracting the naive complete-graph model.
examples.engagement_reason: he recasts ordinary expansion in the framework by taking the naive model to be the complete graph and identifies its adjacency operator with projection onto constants/removal of the top eigenvalue

passes.reason: A complete graph is the maximally edge-dense and highly regular graph used as the naive expansion model, not as a refutation.

examples.name: Unrestricted-interval cancellation with a unique edge length
examples.note: In the simplified case where no vertices are removed, a closed path containing an edge length such as 17 only once has total cancellation as the starting point varies, revealing why single-use labels should be negligible.
examples.engagement_reason: he analyzes the full-interval case and explains why a path with a single uniquely occurring edge length gives total cancellation when the starting point is varied

passes.reason: A labelled path with one edge length occurring once over the full interval is an ordinary cancellation configuration used constructively.

examples.name: Tree-shaped closed path of zero rank
examples.note: A tree-like closed path imposes no nontrivial divisibility relations, exposing the obstruction that forces the later separate count of low-rank or exceptional shapes.
examples.engagement_reason: he places a tree-shaped closed path into the divisibility-relation/rank discussion and checks that it imposes no nontrivial divisibility relations
